output-1.notes: All findings real with accurate line numbers and clean exploits; the os.path.join absolute-path insight (second absolute arg discards the base) is a correct, sharp technical detail. Only dings: mild severity inflation (six criticals) and the enum finding overclaims that the message reveals username existence when in this code bad-password also yields 'no such user'.

output-3.notes: Strong coverage with excellent chaining analysis (path traversal -> reads users.db/source -> recovers secret and hashes) and covers cookie flags; but folds missing-auth inline rather than as its own finding and has a couple hedgy/muddled passages (the executescript tangent and a confused enum rationale).

output-2.notes: Solid coverage and correctly notes the secondary SQLi on the table param, and its enum finding is actually the most technically honest (recognizes the message is identical for both failures); but it entirely misses the cookie HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite finding and its command-injection example payloads are messy and partly muddled.

observations: All three found the core set (SQLi, command-injection RCE, path traversal, forgeable hardcoded-secret cookie, MD5, debug/0.0.0.0, user-enum) with sensible severities and no scope violations (no style/perf/test comments). Differentiation is at the margins: output-1 is the most polished and complete (standalone missing-auth and cookie-flags findings, sharpest exploits, one genuinely insightful os.path.join detail); output-3 matches most of that with the best exploit-chaining but folds a couple findings and is slightly hedgier; output-2 is weakest, omitting the cookie-flags finding and carrying messier command-injection payloads, though its enum analysis is ironically the most accurate about the identical-message behavior. Notably all three flag user enumeration as real when this code returns the same 'no such user' string for wrong-password-on-valid-user, so the true leak is only the echoed username.
